title: Study Guide for Exam #1
text:
Topics
The midterm will focus on concepts and exercises from the following topics:

Data basics

Reading data documentation
Values (e.g. units of observation, types of variables)
Parts of a data frame (e.g. rows, columns, headers)
Missing data

Data visualization

Use of appropriate variables for plot aesthetics
Adjustment of plot attributes
Scales, facets, and themes
Dealing with overplotting
Color palettes
Graphical integrity (e.g. lie factor, data-to-ink ratio)
Proper use of ggplot2
Analysis tools (e.g. measures of spread/center, summary tables)
Recognizing and using different types of plots

GitHub

git vocabulary
Parts of a collaborative GitHub workflow
Addressing and resolving push/pull errors
File organization

Data Wrangling

Six data wrangling verbs
Five join functions (and when to use them)
Problem solving

R

Use of ggplot2 and dplyr
Types of data in R (e.g. doubles, logicals, characters)
Basic statistics (e.g. mean, median)
Operators (e.g. piping, assignment, relational)

title: Study Guide for Exam #1
text:
Exam Rules

You have 75 minutes to complete this exam.
You are allowed only one 8.5 by 11 inch sheet of notes, front and back.
No computers, cellphones, or other internet-connected devices. This exam is closed-note and to be individually completed.
Give your best effort and attempt every question, laying out your thought-process for questions/parts that may be ambiguous for you. Partial credit may be awarded.

title: Study Guide for Exam #1
section: Dataset and Metadata for Practice Questions
text:
Dataset and Metadata for Practice Questions
The nycschools dataset contains 1,829 observations of 11 variables, with the following information for each of the variables:

dbn: district borough number
school_name: name of school
total_parent_response_rate: response rate from parents
total_teacher_response_rate: response rate from teachers
total_student_response_rate: response rate from students
collaborative_teachers_score: average of responses for collaboration from 1-5
effective_school_leadership: average of responses for effective school leadership from 1-5
rigorous_instruction_score: average of responses for rigorous instruction from 1-5
supportive_environment_score: average of responses for a supportive environment from 1-5
strong_family_community_ties: average of responses for strong community ties from 1-5
trust_score: average of responses for overall trust from 1-5

From the NYC Department of Education:

Every year, all parents, all teachers, and students in grades 6 - 12 take the NYC School Survey. The survey ranks among the largest surveys of any kind ever conducted nationally. Survey results provide insight into a school’s learning environment and contribute a measure of diversification that goes beyond test scores on the Progress Report. NYC School Survey results contribute 10% - 15% of a school’s Progress Report grade (the exact contribution to the Progress Report is dependent on school type). Survey questions assess the community’s opinions on academic expectations, communication, engagement, and safety and respect. School leaders can use survey results to better understand their own school’s strengths and target areas for improvement.


The NYC School Survey helps school leaders understand what key members of the school community say about the learning environment at each school. The information captured by the survey is designed to support a dialogue among all members of the school community about how to make the school a better place to learn.

title: Study Guide for Exam #1
text:
Practice Questions

1. Answer the following parts in regard to the nycschools dataset.

Given the context of the data, provide an example of an ordinal that could be included in the dataset.
There are values labeled as -999 in the dataset, indicating a missing value. Describe how we would handle these missing values.
A data analyst wants to remove any data from neighborhoods they deem as “unimportant” despite preparing an analysis about the state of all K-12 schools in NYC. In 1-2 sentences, discuss the ethical implications of these actions.
Suppose we want to include data about the demographic makeup of the school’s student body to the analysis. Assuming we also have the school_name in this additional data frame, describe how we can combine this data into our current data.



2. Answer the following parts in regard to the nycschools dataset.

Suppose we want to look at schools with a trust score over 3.5, create a new column that assigns low to schools with a student response rate less than 50% and high otherwise, and order schools in alphabetical order. Write pseudo-code or code, using the appropriate variables and functions/tasks, to generate these results.
Suppose I want to build a scatter plot that shows the average teacher response rate against the average student response rate, with the trust score color coded. Fill in the blanks to generate the basic plot.


ggplot(nycschools, aes(x = ___________________________________, 
                       y = ___________________________________, 
                       color = ___________________________________)) +
  ________________()


Continuing off the code from the last part, develop a descriptive title and labels for the plot.


labs(title = "________________________________________________________",
     x = "________________________________________________________",
     y = "________________________________________________________",
     color = "________________________________________________________")

The next three parts will center around this particular graph:

ggplot(nycschools, aes(x = Trust.Score, y = highRate, col = highRate)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "Average Trust Score across NYC Schools with Low and High Student Response Rates, 2019",
       x = "Trust Score (Avg.)",
       y = "Low/High Student Response Categories",
       col = "Response\nCategories")









Identify which variables correspond to each of the three aesthetics in this chart.
Provide 1-2 sentences that summarize the results from this chart, using at least one statistic/metric approximately.
Describe one way using pseudocode/code that we can change the colors for the following categories.

Data Basics

Data Basics

Wrong R type for a column

Problematic when running analysis




                mpg cyl vs am gear carb
Mazda RX4      21.0   6  0  1    4    4
Mazda RX4 Wag  21.0   6  0  1    4    4
Datsun 710     22.8   4  1  1    4    1

title: Data Tidying
text:
Vector Types

For an imported data set, this is stored as a data.frame object

Composed of a series of vectors that make up the columns
Each vector is composed of different R types:

double: seen with numeric data (2)
character: seen for written data (“2”)
logical: T, F, or NA
Date: represents a calendar date
Other types as well

May need to factorize a vector as a factor

Used for categorical data

title: Data Tidying
text:
Vector types

Can check the R type for an object with typeof()


typeof(mtcars$am)

[1] "double"

title: Data Tidying
text:
Hierarchy

A vector must consist of elements of the same type

integer, logical, double, character


Question 2: What happens when we create a vector with two types represented in it? Is there a hierarchy to these types?

x4 <- c(2, "times", TRUE, 4)

title: Data Tidying
text:
Testing and Coercion

is.____() returns T/F if an object is of that type

i.e. testing

as.____() converts that object to the specified type

i.e. coercion

title: Data Tidying
text:
Testing and Coercion

Each type has its own function

e.g. as.double(), as.character(), as.logical()
Consider the functionality of each type


Question 3: Use the + and mean() operations on the following vectors:

x1 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
x2 <- c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e")
x3 <- c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)

title: Data Tidying
text:
Special Case: dates

When dates are imported into R, they will most likely be of a character type

Conversion to special type: date
Allows for unique calculations and tasks

Use of lubridate package

as.date(), year(), ymd_hms()
Can find this on lubridate cheatsheet here

title: Data Tidying
text:
Use of mutate()

Whenever formatting columns, we will use mutate to overwrite a variable with a new cleaned up variable.


prisons |>
  mutate(insert new column here)

title: Data Tidying
text:
Setting Dates

ymd_hms() will take a date formatted as year, month, day, hour, minute, second and convert it to a date time format


BeforeCodeAfter



prisons |> 
  select(NAME, SOURCEDATE) |> 
  head(3)

               NAME             SOURCEDATE
1 BALDWIN PARK JAIL 2018/05/16 00:00:00+00
2    CLAREMONT JAIL 2018/05/25 00:00:00+00
3      CONCORD JAIL 2018/05/25 00:00:00+00





library(lubridate)

prisons <- 
  prisons |> 
  mutate(SOURCEDATE = ymd_hms(SOURCEDATE))





               NAME SOURCEDATE
1 BALDWIN PARK JAIL 2018-05-16
2    CLAREMONT JAIL 2018-05-25

title: Data Pivoting
text:
What is Tidy Data?

Every observation has its own row
Every variable has its own columns
Every type of observation unit is a table

Generalizes our understanding of what can go into a cell
e.g. single values, list-columns, tables

text:
Is This Tidy?


        Date Nrthmptn_AQI NYC_AQI Bstn_AQI

2 03/18/2022           69      60       59

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Benefits of Tidy Data

Can allow for different forms through the use of pivoting

Particularly important when

summarizing cases (e.g. wider, left-to-right)
looking at individuals (e.g. longer, top-to-bottom)

e.g. blood pressure measurements at multiple times for same patient

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Food for Thought
Suppose I am collecting music preferences from students in 10 different classes (e.g. genre). Consider these two scenarios for our data:

Every class is in its own row, with each student’s preference as we go across the columns
Every student is in its own row, with their class and music preference in the next two columns

Question 1: Determine which dataset will be “wider” and which one will be “longer.”
Question 2: Determine the pros and cons of using each type of dataset.

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Pivoting Longer

Use pivot_longer() to pivot a dataset from wider to longer format:

You have multiple columns of the same variable/unit you want to condense to a single column

Takes the following arguments:

cols: a series of columns to condense

All other columns will be kept
More common than not to use - to indicate which variables to keep

The names of those columns will become repeated rows in the pivoted data frame, and the values in those columns will be stored in a new column.


names_to: name of column where the column names will be stored
values_to: name of column where the values associated with those names will be stored
Various arguments to support transformations to names

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Example

BeforeCodeAfter




        Date Nrthmptn_AQI NYC_AQI Bstn_AQI
1 03/19/2022           70      72       43
2 03/18/2022           69      60       59





df |> pivot_longer(cols = ends_with("AQI"), # these columns are condensed
                    names_to = "City", # name of new column
                    values_to = "AQI") |> # values for new column
  mutate(City = str_replace(City, "_AQI", ""))





# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Date       City       AQI
  <chr>      <chr>    <dbl>
1 03/19/2022 Nrthmptn    70
2 03/19/2022 NYC         72
3 03/19/2022 Bstn        43
4 03/18/2022 Nrthmptn    69
5 03/18/2022 NYC         60
6 03/18/2022 Bstn        59

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Pivoting Wider

Use pivot_wider() to pivot a dataset from longer to wider format:

Need to have a column that has potential indicators for each of the rows
Need to have a column that has those values for each of the indicators referring to a single item of interest

Takes the following arguments:

names_from: name of column to get the new column names from
values_from: name of column to get the cell values from
Various arguments to support transformations to names

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Separating Columns

At times, our column names or values may be named in a way that can be broken apart systematically

We use separate() to split values in a column into values in multiple columns:

e.g. place_day to place and day


separate() takes the following arguments:

col: existing column to separate
into: vector of names of the new columns
sep: a character (or more) that indicate where to separate columns

e.g. _ in between place and day

title: Data Pivoting
text:
Example

BeforeCodeAfter




  Nrthmptn_3_19 Nrthamtn_3_18  Bstn_3_19 Bstn_3_18
1            70            69 Unrecorded        59





df |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(), 
    names_to = "Measure",
    values_to = "AQI") |>
  separate(Measure, into = c("City", "Month", "Date"), sep = "_")

4 Bstn     3     18    59

text:
Breaking Apart and Creating List-Columns

unnest() will break the list into an entry for each row

has similar functionality to pivot_longer()

nest() will capture all of the elements in a column belonging to shared entries and put them into a list

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
In this lab, we will create a few data visualizations documenting point-in-time counts of homelessness in the United States. Specifically, we are going visualize data collected in 2020 through various Continuums of Care (CoCs) programs. In order to produce these data visualizations, you will need to join homelessness data with census population data and develop and execute a plan for how to wrangle the dataset into a “tidy” format.



Recognize the differences between tidy and non-tidy data
Pivot datasets both longer and wider
Separate and unite columns
Consider the ethical implications of analyzing homeless counts

In order to produce these data visualizations, you will need to join homelessness data with census population data and develop and execute a plan for how to wrangle the dataset into a “tidy” format.

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
Review of Key Terms






Tip



You may wish to reference this Tidy R Cheatsheet when completing this lab.



Tidy data: A rectangular data table in which every row is an observation and every column is a variable describing something about that observation
Pivoting: Rotating data columns so that they are presented as rows or rotating data rows so that they are presented as columns

title: Lab #6: Data Joining (30 pts)
text:
EPA’s ECHO
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is responsible for monitoring and regulating over 800,000 industrial facilities in the United States. This involves regularly inspecting facilities for their compliance with different environmental laws, issuing notices and taking enforcement actions when facilities are out of compliance, and issuing penalties for failures to address violations. A record of all of this activity is maintained online in a database known as ECHO - Enforcement and Compliance History Online. Specifically, ECHO maintains information about enforcement and compliance actions taken in relation to the Clean Air Act, the Clean Water Act, the Safe Drinking Water Act, and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act.
There is so much information available to us via ECHO, and there is even an R package that makes it really simple to access the data in ECHO. However, because ECHO documents compliance with a number of different environmental laws, when we pull data from ECHO about regulated facilities, it is usually organized into separate tables for each law. Today we are going to pull a dataset documenting facilities’ compliance with the Clean Air Act and then pull a second dataset documenting facilities’ compliance with the Clean Water Act.
The Clean Air Act was first enacted in 1963 to regulate emissions from both stationary and mobile sources of air pollution. The Clean Water Act was enacted in 1972 regulate the polluting of U.S. waterways. When a facility violates one of these Acts, it typically means that they have released an excess amount of pollutants, neglected to implement the proper control technologies or standards, or failed to submit reports.

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
Setting Up Your Environment

Run the code below to load today’s data frames into your environment.


library(tidyverse)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3


Warning: package 'tidyr' was built under R version 4.4.3


Warning: package 'purrr' was built under R version 4.4.3


Warning: package 'stringr' was built under R version 4.4.3


Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.4.3

pit <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sds-192-intro-fall22/sds-192-public-website-quarto/main/website/data/pit_2015_2020.csv") 

#Note for future reference how adding the following could remove unreliable counts:
# mutate_at(vars(2:97), funs(case_when(. < 20 ~ as.numeric(NA), TRUE ~ .)))

gender <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sds-192-intro-fall22/sds-192-public-website-quarto/main/website/data/gender_state_2015_2020.csv")
race <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sds-192-intro-fall22/sds-192-public-website-quarto/main/website/data/race_state_2015_2020.csv")

title: Lab #6: Data Joining (30 pts)
text:
Data Analysis

Join Keys
When joining data frames, we need to determine a join key. This will be a variable that exists in both data frames to uniquely identify the data frames’ units of observation. This key needs to be shared across the two data frames in order to be able to join the data frames together. This is because when joining data frames, R will check the value in that variable for one data frame and look for the corresponding value in the key variable for the other data frame in order to determine which units of observation match across the data frames.
For instance, let’s say we have two data frames documenting the same group of students - one documents their current courses, and another documents their living arrangements. For both data frames, the student ID might serve as a join key. This ID uniquely identifies each student, and we expect that that key will be the same in both data frames (i.e. the 99- that represents you in one data frame will be the same as the 99- number that represents you in another data frame.) Because that number matches across the two data frames, we can use that variable to determine which rows (i.e. students) in the first data frame are associated with which rows in the second data frame.






ImportantQuestion 1 (3 pts)



Reference the data dictionaries for these two data frames to determine the names of the variables that we will join on. Remember that these should be variables that we can use to uniquely identify each unit of observation across the data frames.
Write code below to determine the number of unique values for the variables you identify in each of these data frames.

Hint: Remember how we counted unique values in a variable in lab 1.


# Write code here for echo_air_ca
# Write code here for echo_water_ca



If you’ve done this correctly, you’ll learn that there are about 2700 unique values echo_air_ca’s key variable. You’ll also learn that there are about 29000 unique values in echo_water_ca’s key variable.
…but if you look in your environment, you will notice that the number of unique values does not match the number of rows in echo_air_ca, and the number of rows in echo_water_ca. This means that the key variable must repeat, and that certain facilities are represented more than once in each of these data frames.
This happens because even the EPA defines a facility in different ways depending on what laws it is regulated by. What counts as one facility in the Clean Air Act may count as two or three facilities in the Clean Water Act, but there is only one ID to document the relationships between them. This means that the same ID may show up a few times for different parts of the same facility. Check out some of the duplicating rows via my code below. Notice how multiple facilities - sometimes with entirely different names can be associated with the same RegistryID?

echo_air_ca |>
  group_by(RegistryID) |>
  filter(n() > 1) |>
  arrange(RegistryID) |>
  head()

# A tibble: 6 × 8
# Groups:   RegistryID [3]
  AIRName            AIRCity AIRState RegistryID CurrSvFlag ViolFlag CurrVioFlag
  <chr>              <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl>
1 AVCORP COMPOSITE … GARDENA CA       110000474…          0        0           0
2 TEX-TECH ENGINEER… GARDENA CA       110000474…          0        0           0
3 BALL METAL BEVERA… TORRAN… CA       110000475…          0        0           0
4 REYNOLDS METALS CO TORRAN… CA       110000475…          0        0           0
5 AIR PRODUCTS MANU… PARAMO… CA       110000475…          1        1           1
6 ALTAIR PARAMOUNT,… PARAMO… CA       110000475…          0        0           0
# ℹ 1 more variable: Insp5yrFlag <dbl>


Later we will talk about what happens when we join two data frames that both have repeating keys, but for now, we’re going aggregate both of these data frames so that each Registry ID only appears once. Basically, we are going to write code to say: if any of the rows pertaining to this Registry ID document certain kinds of violations, set the violation flag for that facility to 1.






ImportantQuestion 2 (3 pts)



In the code below, for each of the data frames, you should group the data by RegistryID and then summarize by returning the max() value in CurrSvFlag, ViolFlag, CurrVioFlag, Insp5yrFlag for each group. Store the results in echo_air_ca_agg and echo_water_ca_agg respectively. Note how many rows in are in echo_air_ca_agg and echo_water_ca_agg. It should match the number of unique values in RegistryID that you calculated above.

echo_air_ca_agg <-
  echo_air_ca |>
  ______ |>             #group by RegistryID
  _____(                #Summarize the following:
    CurrSvFlag = _____, #Calculate max value in CurrSvFlag
    ViolFlag = _____,   #Calculate max value in ViolFlag
    CurrVioFlag = _____,#Calculate max value in CurrVioFlag
    Insp5yrFlag = _____ #Calculate max value in Insp5yrFlag
  )

echo_water_ca_agg <- #Copy and adjust code above to create echo_water_ca_agg





Joins
So now we have two data frames with unique registry IDs and variables indicating whether the facility has a current significant violation, whether it has had a violation in the past three years, whether it has a current violation, and whether it has been inspected in the past 5 years.
Now we want to join these two data frames together, so that we can check which facilities have violations to both the Clean Air Act and the Clean Water Act.






ImportantQuestion 3 (4 pts)



Write code below to perform four kinds of joins - a left join, a right join, and inner join, and a full join. echo_water_ca_agg should be in the first position, and echo_air_ca_agg should be joined onto it. I’ve started that for you in the commented code below.

#joined_left <- echo_water_ca_agg |> 

#joined_right <- echo_water_ca_agg |> 

#joined_inner <- echo_water_ca_agg |> 

#joined_full <- echo_water_ca_agg |> 



Check out the first six rows of joined_full below and note what happens when a RegistryID is present in one data frame but not in the other. We see data values in the columns associated with the data frame where the RegistryID was present, and NA values in the columns associated with the data frame where the RegistryID was not present. This is how we come to have so many extra rows in joined_full.

joined_full |> head()

# A tibble: 6 × 9
  RegistryID   CurrSvFlag.x ViolFlag.x CurrVioFlag.x Insp5yrFlag.x CurrSvFlag.y
  <chr>               <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>         <dbl>        <dbl>
1 110000473176            0          0             0             0            0
2 110000473247            0          0             0             0           NA
3 110000473345            0          0             0             0           NA
4 110000473372            0          1             0             1           NA
5 110000473407            0          0             0             1           NA
6 110000473434            0          1             0             0           NA
# ℹ 3 more variables: ViolFlag.y <dbl>, CurrVioFlag.y <dbl>,
#   Insp5yrFlag.y <dbl>


With joined_inner, any rows associated with a RegistryID that doesn’t appear in both data frames get dropped. Check out the number of rows in joined_inner.

nrow(joined_inner)

[1] 769


This represents the number of Registry IDs that were present in both echo_water_ca_agg and echo_air_ca_agg.






ImportantQuestion 4 (4 pts)



The three statements below are incorrect. Correct my statements below about the remaining joins.

paste(nrow(joined_full), "represents the number of Registry IDs present in echo_air_ca_agg")

paste(nrow(joined_left), "represents the number of Registry IDs present in either echo_air_ca_agg or echo_water_ca_agg")

paste(nrow(joined_right), "represents the number of Registry IDs present in echo_water_ca_agg")



From here on out, since we are only interested in the facilities with violations to both Acts, we are going to focus on joined_inner. Let’s remove the rest of the joined data frames from our environment.

rm(joined_full, joined_left, joined_right)

You might note at this point that it can be difficult to tell which columns are associated with which environmental laws. This is because originally in both data frames, the variables we are most interested in (CurrSvFlag, ViolFlag, CurrVioFlag, Insp5yrFlag) shared the same names. When we joined these two data frames together, R opted to distinguish between them by tacking a .x onto the variable names for the data frame in the first position in the join and a .y onto the variable names for the data frame in the second position in the join. Let’s give each of these variables more meaningful names. To do so, we can use the rename() function, which is included in the dplyr package in the tidyverse. When piped to a dataframe, the formula for rename() is simple: rename(new_name = old_name)






ImportantQuestion 5 (4 pts)



In my code below rename the remaining columns to differentiate between the column names from the two original data frames.

joined_inner_renamed <-
  joined_inner |>
  rename(
    CurrSvFlag_water = CurrSvFlag.x,
    ViolFlag_water = ViolFlag.x 
    #Rename remaining columns here. Be sure to separate by comma. There are six more to go!
  )









ImportantQuestion 6 (4 pts)



Now that we have a cleaned up data frame, use a data wrangling verb to subset the data frame to the rows where a RegistryID has a current violation to both the Clean Water Act and Clean Air Act. Repeat these steps to determine which RegistryIDs have a significant violation to both Acts. I recommend running this on joined_inner_renamed.
Once you’ve run these codes, open both echo_air_ca and echo_water_ca by clicking on the data frames in your environment. Search for a few of the RegistryIDs that appeared in your analysis to identify the names and locations of the facilities.

# Write wrangling code here for current violations. 

# Write wrangling code here for significant violations. 





Many-to-Many Joins
We don’t know much about these facilities because we lost a lot of critical information (e.g. the facility’s name and location) when aggregating our data by RegistryID above. At the time, we couldn’t aggregate by name because certain facilities sharing the same RegistryID had different names! Aggregating by name would have meant that we’d still have repeating RegistryIds in the data frame - one for each different version of the facility’s name.
The truth is though that we didn’t technically have to ensure that the RegistryID didn’t repeat. We can still join data frames in instances where the join key repeats in both data frames. This is called performing a many-to-many join because we are joining many of the same key in one data frame to many of the same key in another data frame. It’s important to pay attention to what happens when we make this join. Let’s look at an example: the facility with Registry ID 110001181186.

echo_air_ca |>
  filter(RegistryID == 110001181186) 

# A tibble: 3 × 8
  AIRName            AIRCity AIRState RegistryID CurrSvFlag ViolFlag CurrVioFlag
  <chr>              <chr>   <chr>    <chr>           <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl>
1 DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH… CARPIN… CA       110001181…          0        0           0
2 DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH… CARPIN… CA       110001181…          0        0           0
3 DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH… CARPIN… CA       110001181…          0        0           0
# ℹ 1 more variable: Insp5yrFlag <dbl>

echo_water_ca |>
  filter(RegistryID == 110001181186)

# A tibble: 2 × 8
  CWPName    CWPCity       CWPState RegistryID   CurrSvFlag ViolFlag CurrVioFlag
  <chr>      <chr>         <chr>    <chr>             <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl>
1 PLATFORM A PACIFIC OCEAN CA       110001181186          0        1           0
2 PLATFORM B PACIFIC OCEAN CA       110001181186          0        0           0
# ℹ 1 more variable: Insp5yrFlag <dbl>


In echo_air_ca, this RegistryID appears three times in the data frame, and in echo_water_ca, this RegistryID appears twice in the data frame. In each instance, the facility has a different name. What happens to facility 110001181186 when we perform a many-to-many join?

echo_water_ca |> 
  inner_join(echo_air_ca, by = "RegistryID") |> 
  filter(RegistryID == 110001181186) |>
  select(CWPName, AIRName, RegistryID)

Warning in inner_join(echo_water_ca, echo_air_ca, by = "RegistryID"): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 1200 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 1443 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.


# A tibble: 6 × 3
  CWPName    AIRName                             RegistryID  
  <chr>      <chr>                               <chr>       
1 PLATFORM A DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM A 110001181186
2 PLATFORM A DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM B 110001181186
3 PLATFORM A DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM C 110001181186
4 PLATFORM B DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM A 110001181186
5 PLATFORM B DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM B 110001181186
6 PLATFORM B DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM C 110001181186


This inner join created six rows for facility 110001181186. This is because R matched each of the three rows for facility 110001181186 in echo_air_ca with the two rows for facility 110001181186 in echo_water_ca (and 3 rows * 2 rows = 6 rows).
This is one of the reasons why it is important to understand the context of datasets and their units of observation. Here we’ve joined these rows together as if they represented matching facilities. …but just by looking at this output we know that PLATFORM A is going to be physically different than DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM B, and PLATFORM B is going to be physically different than DOS CUADRAS/SOUTH COUNTY/PLATFORM C. The only thing that they all share is that they are sub-parts of the facility with the RegistryID 110001181186. Ultimately the units of observation don’t match across these two data frames because Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act delineate facilities differently. By aggregating to RegistryID above - something shared across the two Acts - we standardized the unit of observation, which made it possible to perform a more meaningful join.






ImportantQuestion 7 (4 pts)



Using my code as an example, determine how many times facility 110000483619 appears in echo_air_ca and how many times it appears in echo_water_ca. How many times would this facility show up if we were to perform a many-to-many inner join for these two data frames? Write your response as a comment in the code chunk.

#Write code here for echo_air_ca!

#Write code here for echo_water_ca!

#Write comment here!





Filtering Joins
Sometimes we want to know which observations that appear in one data frame don’t appear in another data frame. This might tell us where we have missing data. In this case, it will tell us which facilities are regulated by one environmental law and not the other.






ImportantQuestion 8 (4 pts)



Write code below to perform two anti_join()s. The first should tell me which facilities are regulated by the Clean Air Act and not the Clean Water Act, and the second should tell me which facilities are regulated by the Clean Water Act and not the Clean Air Act.

#not_clean_water <- Write code here!

#not_clean_air <- Write code here!









CautionEthical Considerations



In March 2020, the Environmental Protection Agency published a memo that permitted industrial facilities impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic to temporarily suspend mandated pollution monitoring. Research published by the Environmental Data Governance Initiative (EDGI) [@nostMorePermissionPollute2020] has indicated that, during this time, reported violations to the Clean Air Act and the Clean Water Act dropped considerably. However, EDGI’s report goes on to argue that violations were likely being under-counted at this time. …first, because facilities were not being required to monitor and report data to the same degree during Covid-19, and second, because the EPA was conducting fewer inspections during Covid-19. In sum, as they say, “the absence of data should not be taken as the absence of pollution.” What social harms emerge in the wake of these data absences? Who benefits from these policies, and who faces the greatest risks? How should we as data scientists think and act as we anaylze and present this data?

title: Lab #6: Data Joining (30 pts)
text:
In terms of data analysis, this lab has one goal: to determine the number of industrial facilities that are currently in violation of both the Clean Air Act and the Clean Water Act in California. To achieve this goal, we’re going to have to do some data wrangling and join together some datasets published by the EPA. We’re going to practice applying different types of joins to this data and consider what we learn with each.



Identify join keys
Discern the differences between types of joins
Discern what happens when we perform many-to-many joins
Apply filtering joins to identify missing data
Practice data wrangling

Review of Key Terms






Tip



You may wish to reference this Data Wrangling Cheatsheet when completing this lab.



Join Key

A variable shared across two datasets that identifies the observations that will be merged

title: Lab #6: Data Joining (30 pts)
text:
EPA’s ECHO
The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is responsible for monitoring and regulating over 800,000 industrial facilities in the United States. This involves regularly inspecting facilities for their compliance with different environmental laws, issuing notices and taking enforcement actions when facilities are out of compliance, and issuing penalties for failures to address violations. A record of all of this activity is maintained online in a database known as ECHO - Enforcement and Compliance History Online. Specifically, ECHO maintains information about enforcement and compliance actions taken in relation to the Clean Air Act, the Clean Water Act, the Safe Drinking Water Act, and the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act.
There is so much information available to us via ECHO, and there is even an R package that makes it really simple to access the data in ECHO. However, because ECHO documents compliance with a number of different environmental laws, when we pull data from ECHO about regulated facilities, it is usually organized into separate tables for each law. Today we are going to pull a dataset documenting facilities’ compliance with the Clean Air Act and then pull a second dataset documenting facilities’ compliance with the Clean Water Act.
The Clean Air Act was first enacted in 1963 to regulate emissions from both stationary and mobile sources of air pollution. The Clean Water Act was enacted in 1972 regulate the polluting of U.S. waterways. When a facility violates one of these Acts, it typically means that they have released an excess amount of pollutants, neglected to implement the proper control technologies or standards, or failed to submit reports.

title: Project #2 (150 pts)
text:
WarningWarning



Updates to this document are indicated in red.

title: Project #2 (150 pts)
text:
Background
In this project, you will be working in groups of 2-3 to incorporate various data techniques discussed in class on an analysis that you will conduct on a dataset and question of your choice. A Git workflow will be established for all members in your group, and each of you will contribute to the data cleaning, exploratory data analysis, and/or general analysis associated with your research question. The process and results will be presented in a report and another mode of your choice. By the end of this project, you will be able to:

Develop an appropriately-scoped and relevant research question from a dataset.
Apply a manageable Git workflow for all members in your team.
Create clear, efficient, and meaningful code to execute your analysis, including the use of functions, functionals, and tidyverse operations.
Examine visual aspects and metrics from your analysis, including but not limited to plots, tables, statistics, and models.
Summarize your process and findings in a report and creative mode of your choice.

The project will contain the following parts:

Proposal (5 pts; due Friday, 4/10 at 11:59pm in created repository)
Creative Presentation (30 pts; given Monday, 4/27 in class)
Report (40 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm in repository)
Repository (70 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm)
Evaluations (5 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm on Moodle)

Parts of several lectures will be dedicated to working on the project. Aside from these times, you will be tasked with working on this project outside of class.

title: Project #2 (150 pts)
text:
Instructions

Find your groupmates. You will group up in teams of 2-3 and specify this to me in your proposal. You may not work by yourself.
Choose a dataset and write a central research question. Your group will find a reasonably-sized dataset based on an interest of your choice. This dataset should be legitimate with some metadata that explains how the data was collected. To that end, you can find datasets from some of these resources:

data.gov
Kaggle
UCI Machine Learning Repository
NYC Open Data

Once your group has explored the metadata and data, you will develop a central research question that analyzes a collective group, set of variables, or some form of a relationship. From there, you can also develop smaller questions you would like to answer within an exploratory data analysis.
The group members, data source (and a summary of its data/metadata), and a synopsis of your research direction will all be included in a proposal located in the repository your group will create in the next step.
Setup a repository on GitHub and RStudio. One person will need to go to their GitHub profile. In the profile, you will create a new repository by clicking “New” at the top-right hand corner of the screen:

From there, you will put in the following specifications for your new repository:

Then, create the repository. You will need to invite collaborators to your repository as well. Invite all members of the group, as well as Jericho (@jericholawson) to the repository.
Thereafter, the rest of you will create a new project in RStudio based around your new repository. To do so, go to File > New Project > Version Control > Git. Copy the new repository URL and paste it into the first field. Name your project “project2” and place the new repository into a new folder on your laptop. Then, click “Create Project”.
Note: With everyone having access to the repository, it will be extremely imperative to pull before you commit and push. Preferably, you may want to work in a separate branch and then merge the branch at major checkpoints. Refer to lab #2 and Jericho for more information.
Import data and get to know the data set. This will involve a combination of looking at the metadata and a snippet of the data itself. Consider the following questions as you examine the data:

Who is the creator/maintainer of the data?
What do the observations represent? How many variables are there?
How many observations and variables are in the data set?
What are the variables in there? What are the possible values in each variable?
Identify the response or variable of interest in this data set.

This should be numerical.

Are there missing values in the data set? If so, how many and is there a reason why this is the case?

If it appears to be random, you can omit the missing observations. If not, you will need to subset your data.


From this point on, you will be developing 3-4 unique Quarto documents, one/two for each of the next three tasks below. Each of these tasks below will need to have use of each of the following R traits:

Functions
Use of tidyverse
Piping or assigning objects
Comments and/or code chunks

Perform data tidying and possible joining. Your group will need to resolve the following issues that may show up in your data:

File formatting (e.g. .xlsx, shape files)
Missing values
Improper data types (e.g. non-factors, all characters)
Improper column or group names
Multiple files to merge together (either more variables or observations)
Pivoted in incorrect format for analysis

Create a single Quarto document that brings in your original data files and performs data tidying, joining, and pivoting (relating to the above issues and others). You should break this code up into various chunks, labeling/commenting what each chunk does briefly in a few words.
Some data wrangling may need to be done, particularly if you are looking at certain subsets of the data or missing data exists. Your code should properly subset the data so that you are able to complete the data visualizations.
Conduct an exploratory data analysis. With a combination of code related to data wrangling and data visualization, you will explore at least three variables related (in)directly to your research direction that will give your group a better idea regarding the relationships, trends, and characteristics of your data. Unlike the first project, there are no restrictions to what variables you should explore and which tools to use – only as long as they relate to your research question.
You can use any combination of plots (via ggplot2), summary tables (via dplyr), and metrics. If you are repeatedly using similar code elements or chunks, you are encouraged to encapsulate this code within functions and/or functionals. For your report and creative mode, you will discuss what the data indicates about the situation around your research question.
Conduct an overall analysis. Naturally, this will be a continuation of your exploratory data analysis. You will now explore the variables referred to from your research question. At the bare minimum, you will present at least three summary tables, plots, and/or metrics that answer your research question. These figures should…

Provide objective insight into the data related to your question
Spark a discussion about the implications of your results

You will further discuss these two bullet points in your report and creative mode, connecting them to an overall discussion on your research questions and its replications.
You may be inclined to complete further analysis – predictive, cluster, causal, and others. I want to make this clear: this is not required. However, if your group feels that it will aid in a clearer and thought-provoking analysis, you are open to do so (but again, not required!).
Analyze and conclude your findings. Similar to project #1, you will consider the following questions:

What can be said about the data? Trends? Patterns? Characteristics?
What does this say about the situation at hand? How does this further your understanding of the topic?
What are some of the challenges and pitfalls of this analysis and/or data?

Write a report and create a presentation in a mode of your choice. Your group will take everything that has been discussed and create two items from your group’s research. Akin to what was seen in project #1, your group will write a 2-4 page report (double-spaced, 12 point size, a common font). More specifications can be seen in the Components section. Additionally, your group will create an item of a form of your choice, which will be presented in front of the class on the last Monday of class. More information on this can be seen in the Components section.
Develop a complete repository and fill out evaluations. The final version of your repository will be shared with Jericho and will consist of code, your cleaned up data, and a README file. More specifications can be seen in the Components section.
You will also fill out evaluations on yourself and each of your group members, describing each person’s contribution to the project. This will be done on Moodle.

title: Project #2 (150 pts)
text:
Components

Proposal (5 pts; due Friday, 4/10 at 11:59pm via repository)
Your group’s proposal will contain the following:

Names of all group members
Data source
2-3 sentence summary of the data and metadata
2-3 sentence synopsis of your research direction, including your overall question

One person will push this to your group’s repository by the due date. Make sure to add me (@jericholawson) as a GitHub collaborator. No project late days may be used here.
This is completion-based. Your group will get feedback about the feasibility of the data and research question, with approval or revisions from my end.


Creative Presentation (30 pts; presented on Monday, 4/27 in class)
As one of the two items for presenting your research, you will be creating this item based on a mode of your choice.
The mode can be one of the following:

A traditional 3-4 minute presentation with slides
A 1-2 minute TikTok-style reel highlighting the main points
A blog post discussion of what your group found
A 2-3 minute video of your project
A Q&A discussing the main points of your research question

Two critical aspects will be primarily graded on:

(15 pts) Adherence to the stylistic aspects of the mode

e.g. delivery, organization, and setup in a presentation

(15 pts) Content of the presentation

Adequate information to discuss research, including background, your analysis, and implications


This piece will be presented during class on the last Monday (e.g. 4/27) of the semester. Nothing will need to be submitted. You are required to show up for the entirety of everyone’s group presentations on that day. No late work will be accepted for this part.


Report (40 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm via Moodle)
This report will contain all of the analysis and discussion related to the research your group is conducting with the dataset.
The report will be graded based on the following specifications:

(2 pts) Formatting

Contains 2-4 pages of text (excluding images)
Double-spaced, 12 point, common font (with organization)

(8 pts) Introduction

Description of dataset and topic behind research question
Research question(s)

(10 pts) Exploratory data analysis

Figures, tables, and/or metrics of at least four variables
Discussion of characteristics and metrics from above items

(10 pts) Main analysis

Figures, tables, and/or metrics centered around main variable of interest
Discussion of results from above items

(10 pts) Conclusion

Summary of findings
Examination of pitfalls in data and process


This should be in your repository by the due date. Up to two late days can be used on this part (in connection to the repository).


Repository (70 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm via GitHub)
The repository will contain all of the coding work completed. This includes all code, the dataset, and a README.md file (as well as the report). Your group’s repository will have the following elements:

(5 pts) data folder

The .csv file of the tidied data set.

(60 pts) R folder

Three .qmd files

Tidying
EDA
Overall analysis

Code must be working, efficient, and organized.

(5 pts for each file) Working: Does your code work successfully on your data?
(10 pts…) Efficient: Does your code use coding techniques discussed in class (e.g. functions, proper variable names, appropriate processes)?
(5 pts…) Organized: Is your code segmented properly and includes comments/headers briefly addressing what each part does?


(5 pts) README.md file

Title, one-paragraph description of purpose, authors, and an overview of the structure.
Done in a .md or .txt file.

(0 pts) Report (in a .pdf file)

Your group will need to share this repository to me (@jericholawson) on GitHub. This task must be done by the due date mentioned above. Two project late days may be used here if needed.


Evaluations (5 pts; due Monday, 4/27 at 11:59pm via Moodle)
For each member in your group (including yourself), you will answer the following questions in no more than two sentences:

What did they contribute to the project?
Were there any problems working with them?

This part allows me to address issues with group dynamics and fair grading. Points provided here will stem from completion of this task and an equal contribution to the project (informed by evaluations and/or GitHub commits).

title: Data Ethics
text:
Data Science: An Overview:



Interdisciplinary field combining computer science, mathematics/statistics, and domain expertise to extract meaningful information from unstructured data points
Includes the human aspect: use of aesthetics, situational context, and communication to explain the data to the world

title: Data Ethics
text:
Data Is Currency!

“Data is an exchange medium and a measure of worth, much like money. It is exchanged, purchased, and sold in digital markets; frequently, we do not even realize it. Every action we take online—including clicks, likes, and purchases—generates a piece of data. These tidbits add up to a wealth of knowledge that businesses and organizations may mine and profit from.”


Akshitha Vemuru, St. Andrews Economist

title: Data Ethics
text:
Definition

Studies and evaluates moral problems related to data, algorithms, and corresponding practices

Data generation, recording, processing, sharing, AI, machine learning, programming, etc.



Floridi & Taddeo, 2016

title: Data Ethics
text:
Impacts of Data

Data can be collected on just about anything at any given time

Provides stakeholders with a bunch of information
Can be used to train models or generate analysis for good…

Improving service times, identifying lapses in health coverage to certain groups

But can be for bad as well

Predatory loans, personalized ads

title: Data Ethics
text:
Stakeholders

title: Data Ethics
text:
Data Privacy

All about the appropriate management of data, especially involving sensitive information

Sensitive can be subjective

Who should have access to what data?
e.g. credit card scores, dating preferences, phone numbers

Extends to models and algorithms



I will respect the privacy of my data subjects, for their data are not disclosed to me that the world may know, so I will tread with care in matters of privacy and security.

title: Data Ethics
text:

Privacy protections

e.g. encryption, masking

Regulatory requirements

e.g. laws, guidelines

Mitigating privacy risks

e.g. lack of access

For public viewing, street numbers in address data may be masked in some fashion

Front-masked: first few numbers are masked
Back-masked: last few numbers are masked

Done for anonymity

If front-masked, keeps general spacing of address

title: Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)
text:
As you’ve seen in this week’s lectures, much forethought has to go into the creation of a model for artificial intelligence, whether it is from the business, engineering, or ethical side of things. We will focus on the engineering and ethical portions by using a poorly-made example of a natural language processor using Markov chains.
Before we dive into the mechanics of the model, discuss with others around you and write answers regarding the following questions:






ImportantQ1 (3 pts)



Think about the following sentence: “I am having a great day!”. What rules or considerations do I need to follow linguistically in order to convey that sentiment? Is there an order to how those words are being said?








ImportantQ2 (3 pts)



Think about the following sentence: “I am having a great day!”. Are there replacements I can make for each word that would still result in a comprehensible sentence?








ImportantQ3 (3 pts)



Think about the following sentence: “I am having a great day!”. How often am I using each of these words in a sentence? Does it depend on the situation?

title: Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)

title: Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)
text:
Running the Model
Now onto the fun part – we are going to create our own sentences! To do so, run the following chunk below:

# Code to provide the first word of the sentence
sentence = c("the")
wordInd = which(wordData$word == "the")

# We can specify the number of words we want in our sentence here
numWords = 6

# Run this code to create the sentence
for (i in 1:(numWords - 1)){
  wordInd = sample(1:nrow(wordData), size = 1, prob = tranMatData[wordInd, ])
  sentence = c(sentence, wordData$word[wordInd])
}

# Print the sentence here
paste(sentence, collapse = " ")

[1] "the hand have year get work"


There you have it – we’ve generated sentences! But as you see, the results are interesting to say the least. You’ll notice that every time you run it, you will generate a new sentence. Some may make sense, while others may not.
As you’ve probably discussed in previous questions, there are many, many considerations to generating these sentences. The following questions will bring the discussion back full-circle:






ImportantQ7 (3 pts)



Run the code above 5 different times using a numWords of 6. Copy and paste the results below.








ImportantQ8 (3 pts)



What are some observations you notice from the sentences? Are there any similarities? Differences? Confusions? Do the sentences make sense?








ImportantQ9 (3 pts)



What are some factors we may want to factor into our program aside from:

the probability of a word being used, and
what the next word will be given the part of speech of the previous word?









ImportantQ10 (3 pts)



Suppose we attempted to use this model in lieu of more sophisticated programs like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude. What would your perception be? How would this lead to issues with resolving prompts you fed to the programs?

title: Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)
text:
Wrapup
Thankfully, these sophisticated programs are able to answer questions and provide responses that answer our questions, do so in a natural way, and give additional information aside from the initial ask. This is what has made these programs so intriguing and highly used over the last few years.
However, as you’ve seen with this example, developing these models takes a lot of foresight and power. The factors you’ve discussed in this activity only scratch the surface of all potential variables and factors to consider when developing a model. Additionally, we only looked at prompt generation and not into other avenues, such as comprehending prompts.
If you’ve already exhausted a lot of brain power thinking about these factors, imagine the power it takes to factor these considerations into a series of models and executing the results. The data itself will also compound these actions. Also, as you’ve seen here, artificial intelligence relies on the success of the models, data, and processes behind the scenes. Nothing is perfect, and at times, you may find that these models will produce odd, incomplete, or unrelated answers.

title: Lab #7: Components of AI (30 pts)
text:
Prompt
In Slack (under the #discussions channel), provide an example of a time you used an large language model like ChatGPT to resolve a prompt and it generated a rather unusual response. Consider why the response was generated as such.

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
HUD’s Point-in Time Counts
The U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development is responsible for monitoring and addressing housing affordability and homelessness throughout the country. One initiative that they oversee towards this end is the Continuum of Care Program. Continuums of Care (CoCs) are local planning organizations responsible for allocating resources and coordinating services to address homelessness in the United States. Every state has a number of CoCs that report to the Department of Housing and Urban Development.
Every year, on a single night in the last 10 days of January, CoCs required to conduct a “point-in-time” count of sheltered and unsheltered homeless individuals. To generate the sheltered point-in-time count, CoCs coordinate with transitional housing centers or emergency shelters to record the number of people housed in that location on the selected night. There are a few different approaches to generating an unsheltered point-in-time count:

“Night of” street count: Volunteers are sent out to canvass either the entire geography of a random sample of areas in a CoC. While canvassing, they are expected to record the number of people they see currently residing in spaces not designed for sleeping accommodations.
Service-based count: In the 7 days following the night of the designated PIT count, volunteers are dispatched to food kitchens, shelters, libraries and other services identified as spaces that unhoused individuals frequent. There, they are expected to survey individuals to determine if they were sheltered on the night of the count.

Following the night of the count, the collected data points are shipped off to HUD where they are aggregated into the dataset we will be working with today.
There are a number of limitations to this approach of estimating homelessness, which I encourage you to consider throughout this analysis and when responding to our Ethical Considerations question.

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
Our Goal
Today’s goal is to produce just two plots:

A timeseries of rates of both sheltered and unsheltered homelessness by gender for a given state.
A timeseries of rates of both sheltered and unsheltered homelessness by race for a given state.

The first plot will look like this:

…and the second plot will look like this.

To be able to produce these plots, we are going to have to do a considerable amount of data wrangling and cleaning. This lab will walk you through those steps.

title: Lab #6: Tidying Data (30 pts)
text:
Data Cleaning
First, let’s consider each of our data frames. You have pit, which documents homelessness counts from 2015 to 2020 in each state along a number of categories (such as gender, race, sheltered vs. unsheltered, etc.). Note how in this data frame each row is a state, and each variable is a count associated with a particular category.

head(pit)

# A tibble: 6 × 117
  State Sheltered Total Homeless…¹ Sheltered Total Home…² Sheltered Total Home…³
  <chr>                      <dbl>                  <dbl>                  <dbl>
1 AK                           694                    943                      2
2 AL                          1192                   1747                      4
3 AR                           693                    985                      0
4 AS                            NA                     NA                     NA
5 AZ                          3139                   3794                      6
6 CA                         17910                  23981                    148
# ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​`Sheltered Total Homeless - Female, 2015`,
#   ²​`Sheltered Total Homeless - Male, 2015`,
#   ³​`Sheltered Total Homeless - Transgender, 2015`
# ℹ 113 more variables: `Sheltered Total Homeless - White, 2015` <dbl>,
#   `Sheltered Total Homeless - Black or African American, 2015` <dbl>,
#   `Sheltered Total Homeless - Asian, 2015` <dbl>,
#   `Sheltered Total Homeless - American Indian or Alaska Native, 2015` <dbl>, …


This is an example of untidy data. The reason is that there is data we want to visualize (such as race, gender, year, and sheltered vs. unsheltered) that are stored in our column headers, not in cells of the data frame. To be able to visualize this data by race, by gender, or by year we need to pivot our data frames so that the values stored in column headers instead get restored in data cells.






ImportantQuestion 1 (3 pts)



Write code to pivot longer columns 2 through 117 (i.e. all columns except the first state column) in pit. Store the names in column called “Measure” and store the values in a column called “Value.” Store the resulting data frame in in pit_pivoted.

# Uncomment below and write code to pivot dataset. Store the results in pit_pivoted

# pit_pivoted <- pit |> pivot_longer(_____)



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frame should look like this.

head(pit_pivoted)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  State Measure                                                    Value
  <chr> <chr>                                                      <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - Female, 2015                      694
2 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - Male, 2015                        943
3 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - Transgender, 2015                   2
4 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - White, 2015                       625
5 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - Black or African American, 2015   132
6 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless - Asian, 2015                        17


Now you’ll notice that that we have some more cleaning to do because many separate variables are all stored in the Measure column. We have: the year, the demographic, whether the count is for Sheltered or Unsheltered individuals all stored in the column. We need to separate all of these distinct variables into different columns. We’re going to do this in a few steps.






ImportantQuestion 2 (3 pts)



Write code to separate Measure into two columns. One column should be called Shel_Unshel, and the second should be called Demographic. Note what symbol separates these two pieces of data: space dash space (i.e. ” - “). You will need to specify this in the sep argument for separate(). Store the resulting data frame in in pit_separate_1.

# Uncomment below and complete the code to separate the Measure column. Store the results in pit_separate_1

# pit_separate_1 <- pit_pivoted |> separate(_____)



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frame should look like this.

head(pit_separate_1)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
  State Shel_Unshel              Demographic                     Value
  <chr> <chr>                    <chr>                           <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Female, 2015                      694
2 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Male, 2015                        943
3 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Transgender, 2015                   2
4 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless White, 2015                       625
5 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Black or African American, 2015   132
6 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Asian, 2015                        17


We still have some cleaning to do because we have demographic data and year data are stored in the same column.






ImportantQuestion 3 (4 pts)



Write code to separate Demographic into two columns. One column should be called Demographic, and the second should be called Year. Note what symbol separates these two pieces of data: comma space (i.e. “,”). You will need to specify this in the sep argument for separate(). Store the resulting data frame in in pit_separate_2.

# Uncomment below and complete the code to separate the Demographic column. Store the results in pit_separate_2

# pit_separate_2 <- pit_separate_1 |> separate(_____)



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frame should look like this.

head(pit_separate_2)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  State Shel_Unshel              Demographic               Year  Value
  <chr> <chr>                    <chr>                     <chr> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Female                    2015    694
2 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Male                      2015    943
3 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Transgender               2015      2
4 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless White                     2015    625
5 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Black or African American 2015    132
6 AK    Sheltered Total Homeless Asian                     2015     17


As a final cleaning step, let’s remove some unnecessary characters from the Shel_Unshel column. Specifically, let’s remove the string “Total” since we already know that these represent Total counts for these categories.






ImportantQuestion 4 (4 pts)



Remove the string “Total” from the Shel_Unshel column. To do this you should use the str_replace() function. Replace the string “Total” with the empty string (i.e. ““) to remove these characters. Store the results in pit_str_cleaned.

# Uncomment below and complete the code to replace the string "Total " in Shel_Unshel with an empty string. Store the results in pit_str_cleaned

# pit_str_cleaned <- pit_separate_2 |> mutate(Shel_Unshel = _______)



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frame should look like this.

head(pit_str_cleaned)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  State Shel_Unshel        Demographic               Year  Value
  <chr> <chr>              <chr>                     <chr> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homeless Female                    2015    694
2 AK    Sheltered Homeless Male                      2015    943
3 AK    Sheltered Homeless Transgender               2015      2
4 AK    Sheltered Homeless White                     2015    625
5 AK    Sheltered Homeless Black or African American 2015    132
6 AK    Sheltered Homeless Asian                     2015     17


The next issue is that there are multiple kinds of demographics in the demographic column. Specifically, we have both race represented in that column and gender represented in that column. Remember the rules of tidy data, according to @wickhamTidyData2014a:

Each variable forms a column.
Each observation forms a row.
Each type of observational unit forms a table.

This issue violates the third rule. In one table, we have two observational units - a count of unhoused individuals by race and a count of unhoused individuals by gender. To clean this up, we need to separate this into different tables.






ImportantQuestion 5 (4 pts)



Create two new data frames - one for pit_by_gender and one for pit_by_race. To do this you want to extract the rows with values in Demographic %in% the following vector of values: c("Female", "Male", "Transgender"), and store the result in pit_by_gender. Then you want to extract the rows with values in Demographic %in% the following vector of values:
c("Black or African American", 
  "Asian", 
  "American Indian or Alaska Native", 
  "Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander", 
  "White", 
  "Multiple Races")
and store the result in pit_by_race.

# Uncomment below to create two new tables.

# pit_by_gender <- pit_str_cleaned |>
# pit_by_race <- pit_str_cleaned |>



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frame should look like this.

head(pit_by_gender)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  State Shel_Unshel          Demographic Year  Value
  <chr> <chr>                <chr>       <chr> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homeless   Female      2015    694
2 AK    Sheltered Homeless   Male        2015    943
3 AK    Sheltered Homeless   Transgender 2015      2
4 AK    Unsheltered Homeless Female      2015     79
5 AK    Unsheltered Homeless Male        2015    238
6 AK    Unsheltered Homeless Transgender 2015      0

head(pit_by_race)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
  State Shel_Unshel        Demographic                               Year  Value
  <chr> <chr>              <chr>                                     <chr> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homeless White                                     2015    625
2 AK    Sheltered Homeless Black or African American                 2015    132
3 AK    Sheltered Homeless Asian                                     2015     17
4 AK    Sheltered Homeless American Indian or Alaska Native          2015    714
5 AK    Sheltered Homeless Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander 2015     59
6 AK    Sheltered Homeless Multiple Races                            2015     92


Now we have two significantly cleaned data tables. Let’s go ahead and remove some of the data frames we no longer need from our environmennt.

rm(pit, pit_pivoted, pit_separate_1, pit_separate_2, pit_str_cleaned)

With the final data, we could visualize counts of homelessness per state using each of these tables. Check out the plot below to see an example of what this might look like.









The problem now is that we don’t know if higher counts are a result of greater homelessness for that sub-group or a result of there being higher populations of that sub-group in each state. For instance, in the plot above, is the count of homelessness higher for unsheltered white individuals because white individuals are more likely to be homeless or because there is a higher population of white individuals in Florida? We ultimately want to consider the rates of homelessness per x number of people of that demographic in that state. To do that, we’re going to need to join this dataset with some census data documenting population.
I’ve supplied you with two census tables:

gender: documents population estimates and margin of error (moe) for each gender in each state from 2015-2020


head(gender)

# A tibble: 6 × 15
  GEOID NAME    variable estimate_2015 moe_2015 estimate_2016 moe_2016

Note that in these two data frames - just like above - we have values stored in our column headers, and we need to pivot our data longer and then clean it up. We’re going to complete this in three parts.






ImportantQuestion 6 (4 pts)

Step 1: Pivot the columns estimate_2015:moe_2020 longer, storing the column names in “Measure” and the values in “Values”. Step 2: Separate the values in Measure into two columns: “Measure”, “Year”. rm(gender, gender_pivoted_longer, gender_separated, race, race_pivoted_longer, race_separated)

Our next step is to join the census data with our point-in-time count data tables. Let’s just compare pit_by_gender to gender_final to discern the join key.

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  GEOID NAME    variable Year  estimate   moe
  <chr> <chr>   <chr>    <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 01    Alabama Male     2015   2341093  1138
2 01    Alabama Male     2016   2346193  1216
3 01    Alabama Male     2017   2350806  1110
4 01    Alabama Male     2018   2355799  1011
5 01    Alabama Male     2019   2359355  1270
6 01    Alabama Male     2020   2365734  1090


Notice that here, we need to join on three variables: the state, the year, and the demographic. …but you might have noticed a problem. In our census data table, states are written out, and in our point-in-time data table, states are abbreviated. The values need to match for the join to work, so we will need to create a new column with the full state name in our point-in-time count data table. I’ve written the code to do this below. You should run that code before moving on to the next step.

pit_by_gender <- 
  pit_by_gender |>
  mutate(StateName = state.name[match(State, state.abb)])
  
pit_by_race <- 
  pit_by_race |>
  mutate(StateName = state.name[match(State, state.abb)])

Now our data frames are formatted in such a way that we can join pit_by_gender to gender_final and pit_by_race to race_final.






ImportantQuestion 7 (4 pts)



Join pit_by_gender on the left to gender_final on the right and store the results in pit_gender. Join pit_by_race on the left to race_final on the right and store the results in pit_race. In both case, you will be joining by three variables: State, Demographic, and Year. We can set the left key variables to the right key variables by setting the by argument to this in the join: c("StateName" = "NAME", "Demographic" = "variable", "Year" = "Year")

# Uncomment below and write code to join pit_by_gender to gender_final and pit_by_race to race_final. Store the results in pit_gender and pit_race respectively.

# pit_gender <- pit_by_gender |>
# pit_race <- pit_by_race |>



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frames should look like this.

head(pit_gender)

# A tibble: 6 × 9
  State Shel_Unshel       Demographic Year  Value StateName GEOID estimate   moe
  <chr> <chr>             <chr>       <chr> <dbl> <chr>     <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homele… Female      2015    694 Alaska    02      349215   339
2 AK    Sheltered Homele… Male        2015    943 Alaska    02      384160   339
3 AK    Unsheltered Home… Female      2015     79 Alaska    02      349215   339
4 AK    Unsheltered Home… Male        2015    238 Alaska    02      384160   339
5 AK    Sheltered Homele… Female      2016    649 Alaska    02      351559   355
6 AK    Sheltered Homele… Male        2016    846 Alaska    02      385296   355

head(pit_race)

# A tibble: 6 × 9
  State Shel_Unshel       Demographic Year  Value StateName GEOID estimate   moe
  <chr> <chr>             <chr>       <chr> <dbl> <chr>     <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homele… White       2015    625 Alaska    02      484250  1236
2 AK    Sheltered Homele… Black or A… 2015    132 Alaska    02       25022   803
3 AK    Sheltered Homele… Asian       2015     17 Alaska    02       42921   994
4 AK    Sheltered Homele… American I… 2015    714 Alaska    02      101313  1735
5 AK    Sheltered Homele… Native Haw… 2015     59 Alaska    02        8841   428
6 AK    Sheltered Homele… Multiple R… 2015     92 Alaska    02       61755  2190


One final step before we can create our plots! We need to create a new column that calculates the rate of homelessness for this sub-group per 10,000 population of that sub-group in that state and year. You have all of the pieces you need to do this now.






ImportantQuestion 8 (4 pts)



Use a data wrangling verb to create a new column in both pit_gender and pit_race that calculates the rate of homelessness per 10,000 population. Set that column name to homeless_rate. Hint: When creating that column, you’ll need to divide the homeless count by the population estimate and then multiply by 10000. Store the resulting data frames in pit_gender_rates and pit_race_rates.

# Uncomment below and write code to create a new column for homelessness_rate in both pit_gender and pit_race

# pit_gender_rates <- pit_gender |>
# pit_race_rates <- pit_race |>



If you’ve done this correctly the first six rows of the resulting data frames should look like this.

head(pit_gender_rates)

# A tibble: 6 × 10
  State Shel_Unshel       Demographic Year  Value StateName GEOID estimate   moe
  <chr> <chr>             <chr>       <chr> <dbl> <chr>     <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homele… Female      2015    694 Alaska    02      349215   339
2 AK    Sheltered Homele… Male        2015    943 Alaska    02      384160   339
3 AK    Unsheltered Home… Female      2015     79 Alaska    02      349215   339
4 AK    Unsheltered Home… Male        2015    238 Alaska    02      384160   339
5 AK    Sheltered Homele… Female      2016    649 Alaska    02      351559   355
6 AK    Sheltered Homele… Male        2016    846 Alaska    02      385296   355
# ℹ 1 more variable: homeless_rate <dbl>

head(pit_race_rates)

# A tibble: 6 × 10
  State Shel_Unshel       Demographic Year  Value StateName GEOID estimate   moe
  <chr> <chr>             <chr>       <chr> <dbl> <chr>     <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 AK    Sheltered Homele… White       2015    625 Alaska    02      484250  1236
2 AK    Sheltered Homele… Black or A… 2015    132 Alaska    02       25022   803
3 AK    Sheltered Homele… Asian       2015     17 Alaska    02       42921   994
4 AK    Sheltered Homele… American I… 2015    714 Alaska    02      101313  1735
5 AK    Sheltered Homele… Native Haw… 2015     59 Alaska    02        8841   428
6 AK    Sheltered Homele… Multiple R… 2015     92 Alaska    02       61755  2190
# ℹ 1 more variable: homeless_rate <dbl>


If you’ve done everything correctly, you should be able to run the following code to generate the plots presented at the beginning of the lab. Feel free to swap out the State filter for different states to see how the rates compare across the US.

options(scipen=999)

pit_gender_rates |>
  filter(State == "FL") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
             y = homeless_rate, 
             col = Demographic, 
             group = Demographic)) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Shel_Unshel)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  labs(title = "Point in Time Homeless Rates in FL, 2015-2020",
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Homeless per 10,000 Population",
       col = "Gender")







pit_race_rates |>
  filter(State == "FL") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
             y = homeless_rate, 
             col = Demographic, 
             group = Demographic)) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(vars(Shel_Unshel)) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)) +
  labs(title = "Point in Time Homeless Rates in FL, 2015-2020",
       x = "Year", 
       y = "Homeless per 10,000 Population",
       col = "Race")














CautionEthical Considerations



While this is one of the primary datasets used to direct resources for homelessness in the U.S., there are a number of reasons unhoused individual may go uncounted through point-in-time counts:

Only individuals that are visible to enumerators get counted. However, other sources (such as surveys in public schools) have shown that the majority of unhoused individuals are not on the streets or in shelters, but instead are temporarily residing with family/friends or are in staying in motels.
As living on the streets is increasingly criminalized in cities across the U.S., there are reasons why unhoused individuals may try to avoid being seen on a given night.
Only recently have counting protocols started to include separate categories for Transgender and Gender Non-Conforming individuals. When counting PIT data against census population data, however, these sub-groups won’t appear because the census only collects Sex as “Male” and “Female”.
Different CoCs use different methods to generate PIT counts.

For more information see @enationallawcenteronhomelessness2017.
Taking into consideration all of these factors, how should we, as data scientists, think about and present this data? Is there value in PIT count data? How should we go about communicating its shortcomings? How might we go about collecting this data in different ways? Share your ideas on our discussions Slack channel.

title: Functions in R
text:
Dataset for Today

Boston Housing data

via the mlbench library
506 observations, 14 variables

Create a new .qmd file in your submission repository and name the file w11l1_act.qmd. Use the following code:


library(mlbench)
