title: Functions in R
text:
Example: Finding Outliers
Suppose I want to print out the observations that are outliers for a given variable.

Let’s say we want to look at the medv variable. Then,


# Step 1: Find Q1, Q3, and the IQR
quarts <- quantile(BostonHousing2$medv, probs = c(0.25, 0.75))
iqr <- quarts[2] - quarts[1]

# Step 2: Find the bounds
ub <- quarts[2] + 1.5 * iqr
lb <- quarts[1] - 1.5 * iqr

# Step 3: Show the outliers
inds <- which(BostonHousing2$medv < lb | BostonHousing2$medv > ub)
BostonHousing2 |> slice(inds) |>  head(3)

title: Functions in R
text:
Questions

Suppose I want to use the crim variable. Which pieces of code should…


stay the same?
have to change?


What am I showing to the user? What am I doing that would not to be shown to the user?

title: Functions in R
Functions in R
Functions in R
text:
Why Functions?

Removes repetition

Efficiency in code is important!
A function can house the repetitive parts – only the arguments change!

Reproducibility

Can be passed to other users, used for other datasets, etc.

Fewer errors

Less code written

title: Functions in R
Functions in R
text:
Example: Calculating Differences

Create function names that are informative, yet succinct
Default arguments can be included in here

Used to make arguments optional

Lists can be used to return multiple items of different types


calculate_difference <- function(arg1 = 1, arg2 = 0) {
  
  x <- arg1 - arg2
  
  return(list(result = x, args = c(arg1, arg2)))
}

calculate_difference(7, 3)

$result
[1] 4

$args
[1] 7 3

calculate_difference()

$result
[1] 1

$args
[1] 1 0

calculate_difference(arg1 = 7, arg2 = 3)

$result
[1] 4

$args
[1] 7 3

title: Functions in R
text:
Tips

Have descriptive, but succinct function and argument names
Write functions when you are repeating code
Specify names to arguments when calling a function

Ensures you are using functionality correctly
Otherwise, would have to rely on ordering and default arguments

title: Functions in R
text:
Example: Finding Outliers
Suppose I want to print out the observations that are outliers for a given variable.

# Step 1: Find Q1, Q3, and the IQR
quarts <- quantile(BostonHousing2$medv, probs = c(0.25, 0.75))
iqr <- quarts[2] - quarts[1]

# Step 2: Find the bounds
ub <- quarts[2] + 1.5 * iqr
lb <- quarts[1] - 1.5 * iqr

# Step 3: Show the outliers
inds <- which(BostonHousing2$medv < lb | BostonHousing2$medv > ub)
BostonHousing2 |> slice(inds) |>  head(3)

What would be the input?

title: Functions in R
text:
Functionals

R functions that use a function name as one of its inputs

Why? Allows for efficiency in iteration
Can be used on lists, arrays, and data.frames
e.g. map() from purrr, across(), apply()

title: Functions in R
text:
Example of Using a Functional
Say I want to apply the mean function on all columns in this data frame:

BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, zn, indus) |>
  head(3)

  medv    crim zn indus
1 24.0 0.00632 18  2.31
2 21.6 0.02731  0  7.07
3 34.7 0.02729  0  7.07


Instead of using the mean() call four times over, we can just use a map(), across(), or apply() functional:

map()across()apply()



BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, zn, indus) |>
  map(mean)

$medv
[1] 22.53281

$crim
[1] 3.613524

$zn
[1] 11.36364

$indus
[1] 11.13678





BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, zn, indus) |>
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))

      medv     crim       zn    indus
1 22.53281 3.613524 11.36364 11.13678





BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, zn, indus) |>
  apply(2, mean)

     medv      crim        zn     indus 
22.532806  3.613524 11.363636 11.136779

title: Iteration in R
section: Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column
text:
Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column
Suppose I have the following data frame:

BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, nox, indus) |>
  head(3)

  medv    crim   nox indus
1 24.0 0.00632 0.538  2.31
2 21.6 0.02731 0.469  7.07
3 34.7 0.02729 0.469  7.07


Our goal is to find the mean of each column. How would we do this by-hand?

title: Iteration in R
text:
Iteration

Repeats process as we traverse across some collection of objects
The collection?

A list or vector
Columns/rows in a data frame or matrix

The foundation?

For-loops
Vectorized functions
Functionals

title: Iteration in R
section: Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column
text:
Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column

BHsmall <- BostonHousing2 |> 
  select(medv, crim, nox, indus) |>
  slice(1:5)


Look through the medv column


BHsmall$medv


Add up all of the values in the column


sum(BHsmall$medv)

[1] 149.9



Divide by the number of values I have


sum(BHsmall$medv) / length(BHsmall$medv)

[1] 29.98



Write this value down


meanVal <- sum(BHsmall$medv) / length(BHsmall$medv)


Repeat for the other columns


Question: What code will be kept the same? What will need to change?

title: Iteration in R
text:
The For-Loop

Coding structure to repeat code across a set of items
Need the following:

index \(i\) or element from the set
Code that can be repeated for each element in set or in reference to item in location \(i\)
Object to store items as they’re being generated one iteration at a time



Referencing IndicesReferencing Items



# initialized object
for (i in 1:length(data)){
  # do process here
  # alter initialized object from before
}




# initialized object
for (item in data){
  # do process here
  # alter initialized object from before
}

title: Iteration in R
section: Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column By-Hand
text:
Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column By-Hand

Look through the medv column
Add up all of the values in the column
Divide by the number of values I have
Write this value down
Repeat for the other columns


meanVal = rep(-1, ncol(BHsmall))
for (i in 1:ncol(BHsmall)){
  meanVal[i] <- sum(BHsmall[, i]) / length(BHsmall[, i])
}

title: Iteration in R
section: Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column By-Hand
text:
Example: Finding the Mean of Each Column By-Hand

Look through the medv column
Add up all of the values in the column
Divide by the number of values I have
Write this value down
Repeat for the other columns


meanVal = c()
for (item in names(BHsmall)){
  meanVal <- c(meanVal, sum(BHsmall[[item]]) / length(BHsmall[[item]]))
}

While-loops

Loop continues until the condition is not satisfied



x = 10
while(x > 0){
  print(x)
  x = x - 1
}

Wrapup on For-Loops

For-loops in R are inefficient

The language was not designed for for-loops…
but it was designed for vectorized operations

title: Iteration in R
text:
Vectorized Operations

Functions built to complete operations on multiple elements at once

e.g. colMeans(), +, sqrt()
Helpful when there are many elements you want to run operation on

text:
Functionals

R functions that use a function name as one of its inputs

Why? Allows for efficiency in iteration
Can be used on lists, arrays, and data.frames
e.g. map() from purrr, across(), apply()

title: Iteration in R
text:
across()

Applies a function across multiple columns in a data frame
Takes as arguments: the columns to perform the function across and the function name


Example 1Example 2



BHsmall |>
  summarize(across(medv:indus, mean))

   medv     crim    nox indus
1 29.98 0.032468 0.4784 4.162





BHsmall |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), as.character))

title: Iteration in R
text:
purrr package

Included in tidyverse
Package for working with functions and vectors
Provides a family of map() functions
map() functions allow us to apply a function to each element of a list or vector

title: Iteration in R
section: Single Column Data Frames vs. Vectors
text:
Single Column Data Frames vs. Vectors

To extract a column from a data frame, we use pull()


Data FrameVector ($)Vector (pull())



df |> select(var_a)




df$var_a




df |> select(var_a) |> pull()

title: Iteration in R
text:
map()

Applies a function to each element in a vector


Approach 1Approach 2



add_five <- function(x){
  x + 5
}

map(BHsmall$crim, add_five)

  x + 5
}

BHcrim <- BHsmall |> select(crim) |> pull()
map(BHcrim, add_five)

[[2]]
[1] 5.02731

[1] 5.02729

title: Iteration in R
text:
Setting Names

set_names() sets the names of elements in a vector


add_five <- function(x){
  x + 5
}

map(BHsmall$crim, add_five) |>
  set_names(rownames(BHsmall))

$`1`

Iterating Over Multiple Vectors

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Activity: Errors, Warnings, and Messages

Question 1: Collect a list of various errors, warnings, and messages when working through R code. Try to replicate these actions on your own.

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Coding Errors/Bugs

A coding language and its functionality are governed by its rules and restrictions

e.g. cannot add 5 and “g” together, wrong set of brackets to define function

Works in unison with the systems it works with

e.g. computer, operating system, internet

Bugs/errors will happen to users when they run through a program

Within code: we need a mechanism to stop the process and alert users of issues

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Programming Errors

Exist when process doesn’t work due to some aspect of the programming

Syntax, logical, runtime, time limit errors

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Syntax Errors

Code violates rules of programming

Most common error
e.g. [], casing, spelling



x <- mtcars$mpg
mean(x)

[1] 20.09062

maen(x)

Error in `maen()`:
! could not find function "maen"

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Runtime Errors

Unexpected condition encountered when running through code


data <- NA
data[2, ]

Error in `data[2, ]`:
! incorrect number of dimensions

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
How R Deals with Errors

All these errors/problems get funneled into three different conditions

Errors: stops code execution when it occurs

Error message is returned to user via stop()

Warnings: sends message to user when code does not run as intended

Warning message is returned to user via warning()

Messages: purely informational

message()

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Debugging Errors (review)
Examples:

Error in madian(c(1, 7, 13)) : could not find function "madian"
Error in rnorm() : argument "n" is missing, with no default


Key thing here is to read through the first part of error message, and then resolve.

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Common Solutions to Errors

Read through the error!!

Consider processes around location of error (e.g. spelling, functionality)

Making sure everything is spelled correctly and has the right case

R is case-sensitive!

All “(” must pair with a “)”
Break apart code, assigning actions to objects as you go along

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R

Activity

title: Bonus: Testing and Debugging in R
text:
Implementing Your Own Errors/Warnings

To throw an error: use the stop() function in conjunction with an if() statement

Can capture unwanted behavior

e.g. wrong object type, limited functionality

tryCatch() is the more formal way; we will not focus our discussion on this

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Why Analyze Spatial Data?

How are features distributed across geographies, and what does this tell us about potential disparities?
Where are certain events or features concentrated, and what other conditions might implicate these patterns?
What kinds of features are proximate, and what impact might this have?
What is the best way to get from point A to point B?

title: Spatial Thinking
text: Examples

title: Spatial Thinking
text: Geographic Comparisons

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Concentrations of Features

Where are the most Missed Collections on October 1, 2021?

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Projections

Means by which we convert curved surface of the globe to 2D rectangle
Necessarily distorts the surface (e.g. area, distance)
Many projections exist, serving different purposes

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Projections (cont.)




Strebe, Cylindrical Equal Area Projection




Strebe, Sinusoidal projection

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Projections (cont.)




Strebe, Goode’s homolosine projection




Strebe, Mercator projection

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Orange Peel Example



Imagine that you peel an orange

Datum is the original shape of the fruit (e.g. orange, lemon, apple, grapefruit)
Projection is how we go about peeling and flattening the orange





Resource

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Coordinate Reference System (CRS)

Points are in different locations depending on how we flatten Earth’s surface into 2D map
CRS is a system for locating features on a certain map projection via coordinates
Thousands of CRSs but some are more common than others (e.g. WGS 84 most common)
For locations to appear correctly on maps, geographic features and underlying maps need to share same CRS

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
sf Package

Encodes spatial data into geometry objects
Locates latitudes and longitudes according to a particular CRS
Enables setting and transforming CRSs


Lat/LongX/Y Coordinates



nyc_311_lat_long |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 3857) |> head(4)

Simple feature collection with 4 features and 0 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: -73.98817 ymin: 40.72957 xmax: -73.91205 ymax: 40.76753
Projected CRS: WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator
# A tibble: 4 × 1
              geometry
           <POINT [m]>
1 (-73.98817 40.72957)
2  (-73.9193 40.75861)
3 (-73.91206 40.76752)
4 (-73.91205 40.76753)





nyc_311_xy |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("x_coordinate_state_plane", "y_coordinate_state_plane"), crs = 2263) |>
  head(4)

Simple feature collection with 4 features and 0 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 987530 ymin: 205080 xmax: 1008612 ymax: 218920
Projected CRS: NAD83 / New York Long Island (ftUS)

  <POINT [US_survey_foot]>

title: Spatial Thinking
section: How Do I Know Which to Use?
text:
How Do I Know Which to Use?

We hope that it’s listed somewhere in data documentation.
Not always the case.

title: Spatial Thinking
text:
Leaflet

Start by calling leaflet() function, setting the original view, and adding basemap tiles
Note that you need to look up the coordinates of the geography you wish to center in on.




library(leaflet)
leaflet() |>
  setView(lat = 40.7, lng = -100.0, zoom = 3) |> 
  addProviderTiles("OpenStreetMap")

title: Spatial Thinking
Spatial Thinking
text:
Transforming CRS

Why didn’t points appear?
Leaflet assumes that coordinates will be in CRS:4326 (WGS84)
We can use st_transform() to convert points to a different CRS.


BeforeAfter



nyc_311_xy |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("x_coordinate_state_plane", "y_coordinate_state_plane"), crs = 2263) |>
  head(4)

Simple feature collection with 4 features and 0 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 987530 ymin: 205080 xmax: 1008612 ymax: 218920
Projected CRS: NAD83 / New York Long Island (ftUS)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
                  geometry
  <POINT [US_survey_foot]>
1          (987530 205080)
2         (1006606 215670)
3         (1008610 218916)
4         (1008612 218920)





nyc_311_xy |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("x_coordinate_state_plane", "y_coordinate_state_plane"), crs = 2263) |>
  st_transform(crs = 4326) |>
  head(4)

Simple feature collection with 4 features and 0 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: -73.98817 ymin: 40.72957 xmax: -73.91205 ymax: 40.76753
Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
# A tibble: 4 × 1
              geometry
           <POINT [°]>
1 (-73.98817 40.72957)
2  (-73.9193 40.75861)
3 (-73.91206 40.76752)
4 (-73.91205 40.76753)

title: Spatial Thinking
Spatial Thinking
Spatial Thinking
text:
Creating Palettes for Points

colorNumeric(): Maps numbers to colors in a specified palette
colorBin(): Maps numbers into equally-spaced intervals (e.g. 0-10, >10-20, etc.)
colorQuantile(): Maps numbers into equally-sized intervals (same number of observations in each grouping)
colorFactor(): Maps categories into a specified number of categorical buckets

title: Activities from Week 12, Lecture 1: Spatial Thinking
text:
You will use the code below to answer the questions thereafter. The code below does the following:

Loads in county polygon data, bridge maintenance data, and documentation about the types of bridges
Creates a singular dataset with various data wrangling procedures
Removes the original three datasets we loaded in

You will answer the following questions below using Leaflet.






ImportantQuestion 1



Convert nbi_ma to an sf object. The CRS is 4326.

# Code Here









ImportantQuestion 2



Initiate a basemap of MA and add “CartoDB.Positron” provider tiles. You can use coordinates: 42.4072 (lat) -71.3824 (long) and set the zoom to 7.

#Code here









ImportantQuestion 3



Add Circle Markers of bridges to the map.

# Code Here









ImportantQuestion 4



Adjust the radius, stroke, and fillOpacity.

# Code Here









ImportantQuestion 5



Set the fillColor to ADT_029, and swap out the different palettes. I’ve given you code to add a legend. Be sure to update the palette in the legend when swapping out palettes. Which palette do you think is most appropriate?

pal_numeric <- colorNumeric(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$ADT_029)
  
pal_bin <- colorBin(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$ADT_029, 
                    bins = 4)

pal_quant <- colorQuantile(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$ADT_029, 
                    n = 4)


# Code Here

# addLegend(data = nbi_ma,
#             title = "Annual Daily Traffic of Bridges, 2024", 
#             pal = pal_numeric, 
#             values = ~ADT_029)









ImportantQuestion 6



Set the fillColor to STRUCTURAL_EVAL_067, and swap out the different palettes. I’ve given you code to add a legend. Be sure to update the palette in the legend when swapping out palettes. Note how I reversed the scale. Which palette do you think is most appropriate?

pal_numeric <- colorNumeric(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$STRUCTURAL_EVAL_067,
                    reverse = TRUE)
  
pal_bin <- colorBin(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$STRUCTURAL_EVAL_067, 
                    bins = 4,
                    reverse = TRUE)

pal_quant <- colorQuantile(palette = "YlOrRd", 
                    domain = nbi_ma$STRUCTURAL_EVAL_067, 
                    n = 4,
                    reverse = TRUE)


# Code Here

# addLegend(data = nbi_ma,
#             title = "Structural Evaluation of Bridges, 2024", 
#             pal = pal_numeric, 
#             values = ~STRUCTURAL_EVAL_067)

title: Point Mapping
text:
Load-In Data

library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(tidycensus)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(sf)

# Coordinates
nyc_311_xy <- read_csv("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.csv?$select=x_coordinate_state_plane,y_coordinate_state_plane&$where=complaint_type=%27Missed%20Collection%27%20AND%20date_trunc_ymd(created_date)=%272021-10-01%27&$limit=400")

# Rodent Data
nyc_311_rodent <- read_csv("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.csv?$where=complaint_type=%27Rodent%27%20AND%20date_trunc_ymd(created_date)=%272022-04-04%27") |>
  filter(!is.na(longitude)) |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 3857) 
nyc_311_litter <- read_csv("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.csv?$where=complaint_type=%27Litter%20Basket%20Complaint%27%20AND%20date_trunc_ymd(created_date)=%272022-04-04%27") |>
  filter(!is.na(longitude)) |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 3857)
